responses.strategic_rationale: The project must reconcile rapid participant enrollment with high-fidelity data capture while maintaining safety and cost control. Core tensions arise between broad recruitment and event yield, between low-burden monitoring and diagnostic resolution, and between staffing efficiency and regulatory compliance. Balancing these forces requires levers that directly influence enrollment speed, sensor fidelity, workforce design, automation depth, expansion timing, and funding structure. By targeting these levers, the plan can navigate the trade-offs between speed, cost, scope, and quality, ensuring that the residential unit delivers robust phenotyping data without compromising safety or scientific integrity.

levers.name: Recruitment Strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever changes how participants are sourced, affecting enrollment speed, sample diversity, and cost per participant. A broader outreach increases enrollment but may dilute event yield; a narrower, highly screened channel improves event capture but limits numbers.
options: Expand recruitment to include community sleep clinics across Germany, offering remote pre-screening to broaden reach
Partner with patient advocacy groups to identify high-frequency parasomnia sufferers, leveraging peer referrals
Implement a targeted online pre-screening portal that filters candidates before in-person visits, reducing screening burden
levers.review_lever: Controls broad outreach vs. focused high-yield recruitment. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of regional regulatory differences on enrollment.

levers.name: Data Acquisition Fidelity
levers.consequences: Adjusting sensor tiers affects data resolution, participant comfort, and annotation workload. Higher fidelity PSG nights improve event detection but increase cost and participant fatigue; lower fidelity dry-headbands reduce burden but may miss subtle events.
options: Standardize all nights with dry-electrode headbands and supplement with selective PSG only when preliminary analysis flags potential events
Introduce a hybrid sensor array that combines dry-headbands with lightweight, wireless scalp electrodes for all nights
Deploy a machine-learning pre-filter that flags nights for full PSG based on preliminary actigraphy and environmental data
levers.review_lever: Balances high-resolution data capture against participant comfort and cost. Weakness: The options do not address long-term data storage scalability.

levers.name: Staffing and Safety Model
levers.consequences: Changing staffing patterns influences safety coverage, annotation speed, and operational cost. More technicians increase safety margin but raise payroll; fewer technicians reduce cost but risk delayed event response.
options: Adopt a rotating 12-hour shift schedule that maximizes coverage while allowing technicians to rest between nights
Cross-train technicians to perform both monitoring and basic scoring, reducing the need for separate scorers
Implement a remote monitoring system where senior technicians oversee multiple suites via live video feeds, decreasing on-site staff
levers.review_lever: Controls staffing intensity vs. operational cost. Weakness: The options ignore potential burnout and regulatory limits on shift length.

levers.name: Event-Triage Automation
levers.consequences: Shifting to semi-automated triage reduces manual review time but may affect inter-rater reliability. A more aggressive algorithm increases throughput but risks false positives; a conservative approach preserves accuracy but limits efficiency.
options: Deploy a rule-based scoring system that flags only high-confidence parasomnia events for human review
Integrate an advanced statistical model trained on annotated PSG data to rank events by probability, with a human override threshold
Use a hybrid approach that combines rule-based filtering with periodic human spot-checks to recalibrate the algorithm
levers.review_lever: Balances automation speed against scoring accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider the need for continuous model retraining with new data.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Timing
levers.consequences: Deciding when to expand suites impacts capacity, cost, and data quality. Expanding early increases throughput but risks overextending resources; delaying expansion preserves focus but limits enrollment.
options: Expand to 12 suites immediately after the first 15 participants complete their stays, leveraging existing renovation capacity
Maintain 8 suites for the first 18 months, then add 4 additional suites once event capture rates exceed 80%
Adopt a modular expansion plan that adds suites in phases aligned with funding milestones, ensuring each phase is fully operational before the next
levers.review_lever: Controls early expansion vs. phased growth. Weakness: The options do not account for potential regulatory delays in expanding residential facilities.

levers.name: Funding and Industry Partnership
levers.consequences: Choosing partnership models affects resource availability and publication freedom. A pharma partnership provides additional funds but may impose data sharing constraints; independent funding preserves autonomy but limits budget.
options: Secure a conditional industry grant that funds equipment upgrades while retaining full publication rights and a 30-day comment window
Pursue a multi-source grant consortium that combines university, national, and private foundations, avoiding industry influence
Offer a data licensing agreement to industry partners that limits access to raw data but provides analytical support and co-authorship
levers.review_lever: Balances external funding with academic independence. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of changing grant cycles on long-term sustainability.

responses.summary: Add 'implement a remote monitoring system' to Staffing and Safety Model.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project hinges on balancing rigorous scientific data capture with participant safety, ethical compliance, and operational feasibility. Levering participant experience, consent processes, data governance, staffing, technology rollout, and publication strategy directly addresses the core tensions of safety versus cost, privacy versus collaboration, and speed versus quality. By targeting these levers, decision makers can navigate the trade‑offs inherent in establishing a residential research unit while maintaining scientific integrity and regulatory compliance.

levers.name: Participant Experience Design
levers.consequences: Improving the domestic feel of the suites can reduce participant anxiety and dropout, potentially increasing data yield. However, adding ambient controls and personalized meals may raise operational costs and require additional staff training.
options: Design suites with adjustable ambient lighting and temperature controls to mimic home environment for participants.
Implement a pre‑admission orientation video that explains daily routines and expectations, and provides a virtual tour of the facility.
Offer personalized meal plans based on participant preferences and dietary restrictions, coordinated with the on‑site kitchen staff.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant comfort vs. operational cost. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of increased energy consumption from added amenities.

levers.name: Ethics and Consent Workflow
levers.consequences: Streamlining consent reduces administrative burden and speeds enrollment, but may risk overlooking nuanced participant understanding. A more modular approach can clarify participant rights while maintaining compliance.
options: Develop a modular consent package that separates core study participation from optional data sharing process.
Introduce a bilingual consent facilitator to conduct in‑person explanations for non‑German speakers during the admission.
Create a digital consent log that tracks time stamps and participant acknowledgments for audit purposes.
levers.review_lever: Balances thorough ethical review with efficient enrollment. Weakness: The options do not address potential delays from multi‑institutional ethics approvals.

levers.name: Data Governance and Privacy
levers.consequences: Stricter governance protects participant privacy and satisfies regulatory demands, but may slow data sharing and collaboration. Clear protocols can mitigate these trade‑offs.
options: Implement a role‑based access control system that limits data visibility to essential research personnel teams.
Establish a data anonymization protocol that removes all personally identifying information before external collaboration efforts.
Set up a secure, encrypted data transfer pipeline for sharing de‑identified datasets with external investigators.
levers.review_lever: Ensures participant confidentiality while enabling scientific collaboration. Weakness: The options ignore the need for continuous monitoring of data access logs.

levers.name: Operational Staffing Flexibility
levers.consequences: Flexible staffing improves coverage during peak event periods, but increases training overhead and may affect technician morale. Cross‑training can offset these costs.
options: Cross‑train night technicians to perform basic PSG montage assembly and ensure consistent data quality across sessions.
Introduce a rotating shift schedule that allows technicians to alternate between night and day duties regularly.
Deploy a part‑time senior researcher to oversee annotation quality during peak event periods and ensure consistency.
levers.review_lever: Balances staffing coverage with training demands. Weakness: The options do not consider the impact on technician morale during extended night shifts.

levers.name: Technology Integration Roadmap
levers.consequences: Phased integration reduces operational disruption, but may delay full functionality and data completeness. Prioritizing core sensors can maintain data integrity.
options: Prioritize integration of the dry‑electrode EEG headbands with the central data hub before adding environmental sensors.
Schedule a pilot run of the infrared camera system to validate motion‑to‑event mapping and refine accuracy.
Implement a staged rollout of the full PSG montage on selected nights to evaluate technician workflow impact.
levers.review_lever: Guides technology deployment against operational readiness. Weakness: The options overlook potential compatibility issues between legacy equipment and new software.

levers.name: Scientific Output Strategy
levers.consequences: Targeted publication plans accelerate funding cycles and establish credibility, but may limit broader dissemination of preliminary findings. A balanced approach can mitigate this risk.
options: Target two high‑impact sleep medicine journals for methods validation manuscripts and ensure peer review compliance.
Submit a conference abstract on longitudinal phenotyping to an international sleep conference and present findings.
Release a preprint of the event‑triage algorithm results to a recognized open‑access repository for early feedback.
levers.review_lever: Aligns publication goals with funding milestones. Weakness: The options do not address the need for continuous data sharing with the broader research community.

responses.summary: Add 'schedule nightly debrief sessions for participants to discuss experiences and provide immediate feedback' to Participant Experience Design.

responses.strategic_rationale: The residential parasomnia unit must navigate tensions between participant safety, data fidelity, operational efficiency, and financial viability. By focusing on retention, environmental design, annotation workflow, algorithm calibration, funding sustainability, and ethical oversight, the plan addresses core trade‑offs: enhancing naturalistic data capture while maintaining safety, reducing manual labor without compromising accuracy, securing long‑term resources, and safeguarding participant rights. These levers collectively enable the unit to meet its success criteria—high event yield, robust phenotyping, and a validated triage model—while staying within budget and regulatory constraints.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Implementing a robust retention strategy will likely improve participant adherence, raising the proportion of stays that capture at least one adjudicated event beyond the current 70–80% target. Enhanced engagement may reduce the rate of unproductive admissions below the 20% ceiling, thereby improving data yield. However, additional retention activities may require extra staff time and budget, potentially impacting the 3‑year budget allocation.
options: Implement a flexible scheduling system that allows participants to choose nights that minimize disruption and ensures continuity of care.
Adopt a rotating night shift roster that balances technician workload and reduces fatigue while maintaining coverage.
Introduce a real‑time feedback loop that informs participants of their sleep patterns to encourage adherence and self‑awareness.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant flexibility vs. operational consistency. Weakness: The options do not address how to handle last‑minute schedule changes.

levers.name: Sleep Suite Environmental Design
levers.consequences: Optimizing the suite environment to balance domestic feel with safety will likely enhance naturalistic sleep behavior, reducing the suppression of parasomnia events observed in overly clinical settings. A more home‑like design may also improve participant comfort, potentially lowering dropout rates. However, additional acoustic treatment and safety modifications could raise renovation costs and extend the project timeline beyond the initial €750K budget.
options: Incorporate adjustable acoustic panels that dampen ambient noise while preserving a quiet residential ambiance and maintain safety compliance.
Install smart lighting systems that mimic natural circadian cues without compromising the domestic aesthetic and reduce sleep onset latency.
Add modular furniture that can be reconfigured to create a sense of privacy while allowing rapid evacuation in emergencies.
levers.review_lever: Balances naturalistic environment vs. safety compliance. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of environmental changes on data acquisition fidelity.

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Streamlining the annotation workflow will likely accelerate real‑time scoring, reducing the lag between data capture and analysis. A more efficient process may also lower inter‑rater variability by providing clearer guidelines, thereby improving the reliability of event adjudication. However, implementing new tools or training may require additional staff time and could temporarily slow data processing during the transition.
options: Deploy a standardized annotation template that integrates AASM criteria with visual cues for rapid marking.
Introduce a peer‑review checkpoint where technicians cross‑check each other's annotations before final scoring to ensure consistency.
Implement a real‑time annotation dashboard that flags ambiguous epochs for immediate clarification and reduces review time.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation speed vs. accuracy. Weakness: The options do not consider the learning curve for technicians adapting to new systems.

levers.name: Event‑Triage Algorithm Calibration
levers.consequences: Calibrating the event‑triage algorithm will likely increase the precision of episode ranking, thereby reducing the volume of data that technicians must review manually. A more accurate triage may also shorten the time to publication by accelerating data analysis. However, refining the algorithm may demand additional computational infrastructure and expertise, potentially stretching the budget allocated for year two operations.
options: Fine‑tune the algorithm using a cross‑validation set derived from the first 20 adjudicated events to improve sensitivity.
Integrate environmental sensor data to enhance the algorithm’s context awareness during event detection and reduce false positives.
Deploy a continuous learning framework that updates the model with new adjudicated events in real time.
levers.review_lever: Balances algorithm accuracy vs. computational demand. Weakness: The options ignore the need for transparent validation against external datasets.

levers.name: Funding Sustainability Plan
levers.consequences: Establishing a diversified funding strategy will likely provide the financial cushion needed to sustain operations beyond the initial grant period, reducing the risk of mid‑project budget shortfalls. A broader funding base may also enable expansion of participant recruitment or additional research arms. However, pursuing new funding streams could divert administrative focus from core scientific objectives and introduce additional reporting obligations.
options: Pursue a multi‑year institutional partnership that commits to a fixed annual contribution in exchange for data access.
Apply for a competitive national research grant that supports longitudinal studies in sleep disorders.
Leverage a collaborative research consortium that shares infrastructure costs with complementary projects.
levers.review_lever: Controls funding diversification vs. administrative burden. Weakness: The options do not address how to align new funding with existing budget constraints.

levers.name: Ethical Oversight Enhancement
levers.consequences: Enhancing ethical oversight will likely increase participant trust and reduce the risk of adverse events, thereby supporting the safety goals of the residential unit. A more rigorous consent process may also improve data quality by ensuring participants fully understand study procedures. However, additional ethical reviews could extend the approval timeline and delay the start of recruitment.
options: Introduce a real‑time consent monitoring system that tracks participant understanding and flags comprehension gaps to address issues promptly.
Implement a quarterly ethics audit that evaluates protocol adherence and participant feedback and identifies improvement areas.
Create an independent participant advisory board that reviews study procedures and provides ongoing guidance to ensure continuous ethical alignment.
levers.review_lever: Balances ethical rigor vs. recruitment speed. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of enhanced oversight on staff workload.

responses.summary: Add 'Introduce a participant incentive program that rewards consistent adherence' to Participant Retention Strategy.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     First sentence names the core tension. Second sentence identifies a weakness.
     Example: "Controls centralization vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
